concepts.title: Equation of normal
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of normal in different forms

Type 1 : Point Form

The equation of normal to the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ at a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is

$\text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}=\frac{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2\text{a}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

logic : we need to find the slope of normal to find the equation. We earlier calculated the

slope of tangent

i.e. ${{\left. \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right|}_{{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}}}=\frac{2\text{a}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}$

$\therefore$ Slope normal $=\frac{-1}{\text{ Slope }\text{ tangent }}=\frac{-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2\text{a}}$

as ${{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{m}}_{2}}=-1$ for two perpendicular lines.

Hence the equation of normal becomes

$y-{{y}_{1}}=\frac{{{y}_{1}}}{2a}\left( x-{{x}_{1}} \right)$

which is the required equation.

Type 2 : Parametric Form

The equation of normal to the parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax$ at parametric point (at $\left. {{~}^{2}},2at \right)$ is

$y+tx=2at+a{{t}^{3}}$

for proof just replace ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ by $\text{a}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$ and $\text{y}$ by 2at in point form equation.

Type 3 : Slope Form 

The equation of normal of slope $m$ to the parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax$ at the point

$\left( \text{a}{{\text{m}}^{2}},-2\text{am} \right)$ (note that point is also in slope form) is

$\text{y}=\text{mx}-2\mathrm{am}-\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}$

from point form we know eq ${{~}^{\text{n}}}$ of normal is

$\begin{array}{l} \text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} & =\frac{-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2\text{a}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right).....(i) \\ \Rightarrow \text{ m}=\frac{-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2\text{a}}\Rightarrow {{\text{y}}_{1}} & =-2\text{am }\text{(which}\text{ is }\text{the }\text{ordinate of point taken) } \\ \end{array}$

for abcissa we know point lies on parabola hence

hence ${{\text{x}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{y}_{1}^{2}}{4\text{a}}=\frac{4{{\text{a}}^{2}}{{\text{m}}^{2}}}{4\text{a}}=\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{2}}$

hence $\text{ here }\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)=\left( \text{a}{{\text{m}}^{2}},-2\text{am} \right)$

put these values of ${{x}_{1}}{{y}_{1}}$ in (i) to get the required equation

$y+2am=m(x-a{{m}^{2}})$

$\Rightarrow \text{ y}=\text{mx}-2\text{am}-\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}$

Condition for a line to be a normal

The condition required for a line $\text{y}=\text{mx}+\text{c}$ to be a normal to the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ is

$\text{c}=-2\mathrm{am}-\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}$

Logic : Nothing just compare the equation of normal derived just above in slope form with

$\text{y}=\text{mx}+\text{c}$ to arrive at the result.

Point of intersection of the normal to the parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax$ at $\left( at_{1}^{2} \right.$, $\left. 2a{{t}_{1}} \right)$ and 

$\left( \text{at}_{2}^{2},2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right)$ : 

Equation of normal at $\text{A}\left( \text{at}_{1}^{2},2\text{at}{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\text{B}\left( \text{at}_{2}^{2},\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right)$ are

$\begin{matrix} \text{y}+{{\text{t}}_{1}}\text{x}=2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{1}^2}+a\text{t}_{1}^{3} \\ \text{y}+{{\text{t}}_{2}}\text{x}=2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{2}}+a\text{t}_{2}^{3} \\ \left( 1 \right)-\left( 2 \right)\Rightarrow \left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}-{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right)\text{x}=2\text{a}\left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}-{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right)+\text{a}\left( \text{t}_{1}^{3}-\text{t}_{2}^{3} \right) \\ \Rightarrow \text{x}=2\text{a}+\text{a}\left( \text{t}_{1}^{2}+\text{t}_{2}^{2}+{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right) \\ {{\text{t}}_{2}}\cdot \left( 1 \right)-{{\text{t}}_{1}}\left( 2 \right)\Rightarrow \left( {{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)\text{y}=a{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}\left( \text{t}_{1}^{2}-{{\text{t}}_{2}}{{~}^{2}} \right) \\ \Rightarrow \text{y}=-a{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

Thus point of intersection of lines (1) and (2) is

$\left( 2a+a\left( t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}+{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}} \right),-a{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}\left( {{t}_{1}}+{{t}_{2}} \right) \right)$

concepts.title: Number of normals from a single point
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation between ' ${{t}_{1}}$ ' and ' ${{t}_{2}}$ ' if normal at ' ${{t}_{1}}$ ' meets the parabola again at ' ${{t}_{2}}$ ' 

Let the parabola be ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$, equation of normal at $\text{P}\left( \text{at}_{1}^{2},2\text{at}{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)$ is

$y=-{{t}_{1}}x+2a{{t}_{1}}+at_{1}^{3}$ …..(1)

Since it meet the parabola again at $\text{Q}\left( \text{at}_{2}^{2},2a{{t}_{2}} \right)$ then eq (1) passes through $\text{Q}\left( \text{at}_{2}^{2},2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right)$

 

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \therefore 2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{2}}=-\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{1}}\text{t}_{2}^{2}+2\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{1}}+\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{}^{3}} \\ {} & \Rightarrow 2\text{a}\left( {{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)+\text{a}{{\text{t}}_{1}}\left( \text{t}_{2}^{2}-\text{t}_{1}^{2} \right)=0 \\ {} & \Rightarrow a\left( {{t}_{2}}-{{t}_{1}} \right)\left[ 2+{{t}_{1}}\left( {{t}_{2}}+{{t}_{1}} \right) \right]=0 \\ {} & \text{. }a\left( {{t}_{2}}-{{t}_{1}} \right)\ne 0~\left( .{{t}_{1}}\text{ and }{{t}_{2}}\text{ are }\text{ different} \right) \\ {} & \therefore 2+{{t}_{1}}\left( {{t}_{2}}+{{t}_{1}} \right)=0 \\ {} & \therefore {{\text{t}}_{2}}=-{{\text{t}}_{1}}-\frac{2}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}} \\ \end{array}$

Result 2 : If normal at ' ${{\text{t}}_{1}}$ ' and ' ${{\text{t}}_{2}}$ ' meets the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ at same point then

${{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}=2$

Proof : Suppose normal’s meet at 'T' then

$\begin{array}{l} \text{T} ~=-{{\text{t}}_{1}}-\frac{2}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}}=-{{\text{t}}_{2}}-\frac{2}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}} \\ \left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}-{{\text{t}}_{2}} \right) ~=2\left( \frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}} \right) \\ {{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}} ~=2 \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Condition for three normals
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Theorem

From any point three normals can be drawn to parabola of which at least one will be real 

Let the equation of the parabola be ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ and let the given point be $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$.

The equation of any normal to the parabola in the slope form is

$\text{y}=\text{mx}-2\text{am}-\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}$

If this normal passes through $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$, then

${{\text{y}}_{1}}=\text{m}{{\text{x}}_{1}}-2\text{am}-\text{a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}\text{ or }\text{ a}{{\text{m}}^{3}}+\left( 2\text{a}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\text{m}+{{\text{y}}_{1}}=0.$

This is a cubic equation in $\text{m}$ and has three roots, say ${{\text{m}}_{1}},{{\text{m}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{m}}_{3}}$.

Corresponding to these three values of $\text{m}$ we will get three normal’s to the parabola passing through $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$.

Since imaginary roots of a polynomial equation with real coefficients always occur in pair, therefore at least one root will be real and hence at least one normal will be real.

Note : Any three points on a parabola normals at which pass through a common point are called co-normal points.
